content:
# Leviticus 13 — Disagreement Log

**Chapter:** Leviticus 13 (59 verses)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Agents:** EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic)
**Total disagreements:** 54

---

## Summary

Leviticus 13 is the longest chapter in Leviticus and produces the highest disagreement count of any chapter to date. The 54 logged disagreements fall into five major patterns:

1. **Tzara'at terminology** (most pervasive): EMET preserves tzara'at untranslated throughout; LOGOS renders it "skin disease"; PNEUMA renders it "skin disease." The project ruling preserves tzara'at untranslated. This pattern recurs at nearly every occurrence of the term across all 59 verses and generates the largest single cluster of disagreements.

2. **Nega / nega tzara'at rendering**: EMET: "mark of tzara'at." LOGOS: "outbreak of skin disease." PNEUMA: "case of skin disease." Consensus: "mark of tzara'at."

3. **Pronoun number / gender inclusivity**: PNEUMA systematically shifts singular masculine Hebrew pronouns to plural ("they/them/their") for gender inclusivity. EMET and LOGOS preserve MT singular forms. Consensus follows MT singular.

4. **Technical hapax legomena** (neteq, bohaq, petechet, tsarevel, mam'eret): EMET preserves the Hebrew terms; LOGOS and PNEUMA translate them into English equivalents. Consensus preserves key hapax terms at first occurrence and uses English equivalents where readability demands.

5. **Structural/informational additions**: PNEUMA frequently adds explanatory words ("only," "further," "already") not present in the MT. LOGOS occasionally restructures clause order. Consensus follows MT structure.

---

## Disagreement 1 — V. 1: Name forms (Moses and Aaron)
**Verse:** 1
**Hebrew:** מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן (Moshe ve-Aharon)
**EMET:** "Moshe and Aharon"
**LOGOS:** "Moses and Aaron"
**PNEUMA:** "Moses and Aaron"
**Ruling:** "Moses and Aaron"
**Type:** Name convention
**Notes:** EMET uses Hebrew transliterations throughout its literal register; consensus follows project convention of English name forms. This applies to all subsequent occurrences of these names.

---

## Disagreement 2 — V. 2: "person" vs. "someone"
**Verse:** 2
**Hebrew:** אָדָם (adam)
**EMET:** "a person [adam]"
**LOGOS:** "a person"
**PNEUMA:** "someone"
**Ruling:** "a person"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Adam is the generic Hebrew term for a human being. "A person" preserves the generic legal character of the term in its priestly-legislative context. "Someone" (PNEUMA) is too informal for the legislative register.

---

## Disagreement 3 — V. 2: sapachat rendering
**Verse:** 2
**Hebrew:** סַפַּחַת (sapachat)
**EMET:** "a rash"
**LOGOS:** "a scab"
**PNEUMA:** "a scab"
**Ruling:** "a rash"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Sapachat derives from sapach ("to adhere, to join onto"), suggesting an adherent, spreading, flat lesion — a rash-type presentation rather than a dry crusted scab. "Rash" (EMET) better captures the spreading, skin-surface character of sapachat. LOGOS and PNEUMA's "scab" implies a dry, crusted healing lesion, which does not fit the context where sapachat requires the same priestly examination as the other two conditions.

---

## Disagreement 4 — V. 2: "bright spot" vs. "shiny spot"
**Verse:** 2
**Hebrew:** בַּהֶרֶת (baheret)
**EMET:** "a bright-spot"
**LOGOS:** "a bright spot"
**PNEUMA:** "a shiny spot"
**Ruling:** "a bright spot"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Baheret derives from bahar ("to be bright, to shine"). EMET and LOGOS agree on "bright spot"; PNEUMA uses "shiny spot." "Bright spot" is the established project rendering and better reflects the visual distinctiveness (a pale, bright patch against normal skin color) rather than merely a surface-gloss quality. "Shiny" suggests reflectiveness; "bright" suggests luminosity or whiteness, which is more accurate to the diagnostic presentation.

---

## Disagreement 5 — V. 2: "mark of tzara'at" vs. "outbreak of skin disease" vs. "case of skin disease"
**Verse:** 2 (and throughout, vv. 3, 8, 9, 20, 22, 25, 27, 29, 42, 44, 47, 49, 50, 51)
**Hebrew:** נֶגַע צָרַעַת (nega tzara'at)
**EMET:** "mark of tzara'at"
**LOGOS:** "outbreak of skin disease"
**PNEUMA:** "case of skin disease"
**Ruling:** "mark of tzara'at"
**Type:** Word choice / Interpretive
**Notes:** This is the most pervasive disagreement in the chapter. The technical compound nega tzara'at uses nega ("mark, stroke, blow") as the primary noun; tzara'at is the type of condition. "Mark" preserves the forensic precision of nega. "Outbreak" (LOGOS) and "case" (PNEUMA) both interpret rather than render the Hebrew, and both require substituting "skin disease" for tzara'at, which the project ruling forbids. This ruling applies to all occurrences throughout the chapter.

---

## Disagreement 6 — V. 2: tzara'at (standalone occurrences throughout)
**Verse:** 2 and throughout (vv. 3, 8, 11, 12, 13, 15, 20, 22, 25, 27, 30, 42, 43, 44, 47, 51, 52, 59, etc.)
**Hebrew:** צָרַעַת (tzara'at)
**EMET:** "tzara'at" (untranslated throughout)
**LOGOS:** "skin disease" throughout
**PNEUMA:** "skin disease" throughout
**Ruling:** "tzara'at" (preserved untranslated)
**Type:** Word choice / Interpretive
**Notes:** The project ruling is explicit: tzara'at is preserved untranslated. LOGOS and PNEUMA both translate the term as "skin disease." While "skin disease" is an accurate gloss for the ritual category, the project has determined that preserving the term (as with ketonet passim, massebah, niddah, El Shaddai, etc.) is the correct approach for Levitical technical vocabulary. This disagreement recurs at virtually every occurrence of tzara'at throughout the chapter.

---

## Disagreement 7 — V. 2: "he shall be brought" — passive vs. active / number
**Verse:** 2
**Hebrew:** וְהוּבָא (ve-huva — niphal passive)
**EMET:** "he shall be brought"
**LOGOS:** "he shall be brought"
**PNEUMA:** "they shall be brought"
**Ruling:** "he shall be brought"
**Type:** Structure / Word choice
**Notes:** PNEUMA systematically shifts the Hebrew singular masculine pronoun to plural ("they/them") throughout the chapter for gender inclusivity. This pattern recurs across vv. 2–46. The MT consistently uses masculine singular forms in the primary diagnostic unit (adam, "a person," is the subject — grammatically masculine singular in Hebrew). Consensus follows the MT singular; gender inclusivity is handled through adam's universal application ("a person") rather than by shifting grammatical number.

---

## Disagreement 8 — V. 3: "skin of his flesh" vs. "skin of his body"
**Verse:** 3 (and vv. 4, 38, 39)
**Hebrew:** עוֹר בְּשָׂרוֹ ('or basar)
**EMET:** "the skin of his flesh"
**LOGOS:** "the skin of his body"
**PNEUMA:** "the surrounding skin"
**Ruling:** "the skin of his flesh"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** 'Or basar is a specific compound: 'or ("skin") + basar ("flesh, body tissue"). The phrase is used consistently throughout Leviticus 13 as a technical diagnostic locator. "His flesh" (EMET) is more precise than "his body" (LOGOS) since basar specifically means flesh/tissue, not merely body in the abstract. PNEUMA's "surrounding skin" is interpretive and introduces a comparative dimension not in the Hebrew at these occurrences.

---

## Disagreement 9 — V. 3: declarative verdict phrase structure
**Verse:** 3
**Hebrew:** נֶגַע צָרַעַת הוּא (nega tzara'at hu')
**EMET:** "it is a mark of tzara'at"
**LOGOS:** "it is an outbreak of skin disease"
**PNEUMA:** "it is a case of skin disease"
**Ruling:** "it is a mark of tzara'at"
**Type:** Word choice / Interpretive
**Notes:** Follows the ruling at Disagreement 5 above. The nominal sentence with hu' as copula is the formal priestly verdict formula. The consensus preserves the exact technical terms.

---

## Disagreement 10 — V. 4: object of hisgir ("shut up")
**Verse:** 4
**Hebrew:** וְהִסְגִּיר הַכֹּהֵן אֶת-הַנֶּגַע (ve-hisgir ha-kohen et-ha-nega)
**EMET:** "the priest shall shut up the mark"
**LOGOS:** "the priest shall shut up the diseased person"
**PNEUMA:** "the priest shall isolate the affected person"
**Ruling:** "the priest shall shut up the mark"
**Type:** Structure / Word choice
**Notes:** The MT's direct object is et-ha-nega ("the mark"), not the person. LOGOS shifts the object to "the diseased person"; PNEUMA uses "isolate" for hisgir and "the affected person" as object. "Shut up" (from sagar, "to close, to confine") is the established project rendering for hisgir and should be retained. The MT syntax — where the mark is what is "shut up" (under observation) — is preserved. This ruling applies to all hisgir occurrences (vv. 4, 5, 11, 21, 26, 31, 33, 50, 54).

---

## Disagreement 11 — V. 5: "has remained unchanged" vs. "is the same" vs. "looks the same"
**Verse:** 5
**Hebrew:** עָמַד בְּעֵינָיו (amad be-'eynav)
**EMET:** "has remained as-it-was"
**LOGOS:** "the outbreak is the same"
**PNEUMA:** "the sore looks the same"
**Ruling:** "has remained unchanged"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Amad be-'eynav = "stood in his eyes" — the idiomatic expression for a lesion that has not changed in the priest's visual assessment. "Has remained unchanged" is the clearest idiomatic English equivalent without over-literalizing "as-it-was" (EMET) or under-rendering with "is the same" (LOGOS) or "looks the same" (PNEUMA, which introduces a mere visual impression).

---

## Disagreement 12 — V. 5: hisgir object and "isolate" language
**Verse:** 5
**Hebrew:** וְהִסְגִּיר הַכֹּהֵן אֹתוֹ (ve-hisgir ha-kohen oto)
**EMET:** "the priest shall shut him up"
**LOGOS:** "the priest shall shut him up"
**PNEUMA:** "the priest shall isolate them"
**Ruling:** "the priest shall shut him up"
**Type:** Word choice / Structure
**Notes:** Same as Disagreement 10: PNEUMA uses "isolate" and pluralizes. Consensus retains "shut him up" and the MT singular.

---

## Disagreement 13 — V. 6: "has faded" vs. "has dimmed"
**Verse:** 6 (and vv. 21, 26, 28, 56)
**Hebrew:** כֵּהָה (kehah)
**EMET:** "has dimmed"
**LOGOS:** "has faded"
**PNEUMA:** "has faded"
**Ruling:** "has faded"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Kehah (from kahah, "to be faint, to grow dim, to fade") describes a lesion that has lost intensity or vivid coloration. "Faded" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is the more natural English dermatological description; "dimmed" (EMET) is accurate but slightly awkward in a skin-condition context.

---

## Disagreement 14 — V. 6: mispeachat rendering
**Verse:** 6 (and v. 7, 8)
**Hebrew:** מִסְפַּחַת (mispeachat)
**EMET:** "a rash"
**LOGOS:** "a scab"
**PNEUMA:** "a scab" (with "only a scab")
**Ruling:** "a rash"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Same reasoning as Disagreement 3 (sapachat). Mispeachat is related to sapachat (same root), denoting a faded, non-spreading lesion reclassified as benign. "Rash" is preferred over "scab" for the same reasons. PNEUMA adds "only" beyond the MT ("only a scab"), which introduces an evaluative qualification not present in the Hebrew.

---

## Disagreement 15 — V. 7: "spreads on the skin" vs. "spreads widely" vs. "spreads further"
**Verse:** 7
**Hebrew:** פָּשׂוֹ תִפְשֶׂה (pasoh tifshah — infinitive absolute + finite verb)
**EMET:** "spreads widely [pasoh tifshah]"
**LOGOS:** "spreads on the skin"
**PNEUMA:** "spreads further"
**Ruling:** "spreads on the skin"
**Type:** Word choice / Structure
**Notes:** The infinitive-absolute construction (pasoh tifshah) is emphatic in Hebrew, meaning "spreading it shall spread." EMET's "spreads widely" captures the emphasis. LOGOS simplifies to "spreads on the skin." PNEUMA adds "further" (comparative, not in the MT). Consensus: "spreads on the skin" — the location qualifier is present in the MT clause (ba-'or, "on the skin"), and the spreading itself is the key criterion; English does not need to double the verb to convey the emphasis.

---

## Disagreement 16 — V. 8: verdict formula "it is tzara'at" vs. "it is a skin disease"
**Verse:** 8
**Hebrew:** צָרַעַת הִיא (tzara'at hi')
**EMET:** "it is tzara'at"
**LOGOS:** "it is a skin disease"
**PNEUMA:** "it is a skin disease"
**Ruling:** "it is tzara'at"
**Type:** Word choice / Interpretive
**Notes:** The bare nominal verdict tzara'at hi' is the formal declaratory sentence of unclean status. Preserving "tzara'at" in the verdict is essential; rendering it "skin disease" interprets the term. This pattern recurs at every verdict occurrence (vv. 8, 11, 15, 20, 22, 25, etc.).

---

## Disagreement 17 — V. 9: "mark of tzara'at" vs. "skin disease" (opening of new section)
**Verse:** 9
**Hebrew:** נֶגַע צָרַעַת (nega tzara'at)
**EMET:** "a mark of tzara'at"
**LOGOS:** "a skin disease"
**PNEUMA:** "a skin disease"
**Ruling:** "a mark of tzara'at"
**Type:** Word choice / Interpretive
**Notes:** Same as Disagreement 5.

---

## Disagreement 18 — V. 11: "chronic tzara'at" vs. "chronic skin disease"
**Verse:** 11
**Hebrew:** צָרַעַת נוֹשֶׁנֶת (tzara'at noshenet)
**EMET:** "a chronic tzara'at"
**LOGOS:** "a chronic skin disease"
**PNEUMA:** "a chronic skin disease"
**Ruling:** "a chronic tzara'at"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Noshenet ("old, long-standing, chronic") is well captured by "chronic." The preserved term tzara'at is required by project ruling; LOGOS and PNEUMA both substitute "skin disease."

---

## Disagreement 19 — V. 11: "He shall not shut him up"
**Verse:** 11
**Hebrew:** לֹא יַסְגִּרֶנּוּ (lo' yasgirenu)
**EMET:** "He shall not shut him up"
**LOGOS:** "He shall not shut him up"
**PNEUMA:** "he need not isolate them"
**Ruling:** "He shall not shut him up"
**Type:** Word choice / Structure
**Notes:** PNEUMA uses "isolate" and pluralizes the pronoun. Consensus preserves "shut him up" for consistency with the hisgir formula throughout the chapter, and retains the MT singular pronoun.

---

## Disagreement 20 — V. 12: "breaks out extensively" vs. "breaks out widely" vs. "breaks out all over"
**Verse:** 12
**Hebrew:** פָּרוֹחַ תִּפְרַח (paroach tifrach — infinitive absolute)
**EMET:** "breaks out wildly"
**LOGOS:** "breaks out widely"
**PNEUMA:** "breaks out all over"
**Ruling:** "breaks out extensively"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** All three agents disagree on the English modifier for the emphatic infinitive-absolute construction. "Wildly" (EMET) over-emotionalizes; "widely" (LOGOS) is acceptable but understated; "all over" (PNEUMA) anticipates the subsequent clause (covering all the skin) rather than translating the verb's emphasis alone. "Extensively" captures the completeness of the outbreak without emotional or anticipatory coloring.

---

## Disagreement 21 — V. 13: object of "declare clean"
**Verse:** 13 (and v. 17)
**Hebrew:** וְטִהַר הַכֹּהֵן אֶת-הַנֶּגַע (ve-tihar ha-kohen et-ha-nega)
**EMET:** "he shall declare clean the mark"
**LOGOS:** "he shall pronounce him clean of the outbreak"
**PNEUMA:** "he shall declare the afflicted person clean"
**Ruling:** "he shall declare the mark clean"
**Type:** Structure
**Notes:** The MT's direct object is et-ha-nega ("the mark"). LOGOS shifts to "him" and adds "of the outbreak"; PNEUMA shifts to "the afflicted person." EMET is syntactically correct but the English word order is inverted. Consensus: "he shall declare the mark clean" — restoring natural English order while preserving the MT's object.

---

## Disagreement 22 — V. 17: "declare the mark clean" vs. "pronounce the diseased person clean"
**Verse:** 17
**Hebrew:** וְטִהַר הַכֹּהֵן אֶת-הַנֶּגַע (ve-tihar ha-kohen et-ha-nega)
**EMET:** "the priest shall declare the mark clean"
**LOGOS:** "the priest shall pronounce the diseased person clean"
**PNEUMA:** "the priest shall declare the afflicted person clean"
**Ruling:** "the priest shall declare the mark clean"
**Type:** Structure
**Notes:** Same as Disagreement 21. The object is et-ha-nega ("the mark"), which EMET preserves. LOGOS and PNEUMA substitute "the diseased person" or "the afflicted person."

---

## Disagreement 23 — V. 18: subject phrasing for boil-section opening
**Verse:** 18
**Hebrew:** וּבָשָׂר כִּי-יִהְיֶה בוֹ בְעֹרוֹ שְׁחִין (u-vasar ki yihyeh vo be-oro shechim)
**EMET:** "And flesh when there is in it in its skin a boil"
**LOGOS:** "And when there is on the skin of one's body a boil"
**PNEUMA:** "When someone has a boil on their skin"
**Ruling:** "And when there is on the skin of a body a boil"
**Type:** Structure / Word choice
**Notes:** The MT has a somewhat awkward syntax: "and flesh, when there is in it, in its skin, a boil." The consensus follows LOGOS's restructuring for natural English while preserving "a body" rather than "one's body" (more impersonal) and avoiding PNEUMA's personalizing shift to "someone."

---

## Disagreement 24 — V. 19: "reddish-white bright spot" vs. "reddish-white shiny spot"
**Verse:** 19 (and vv. 24, 42)
**Hebrew:** בַּהֶרֶת לְבָנָה אַדְמְדֶּמֶת (baheret levana admedenet)
**EMET:** "a bright reddish-white spot"
**LOGOS:** "a reddish-white bright spot"
**PNEUMA:** "a reddish-white shiny spot"
**Ruling:** "a reddish-white bright spot"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** PNEUMA replaces "bright" with "shiny" for baheret, departing from the established "bright spot" rendering. The compound color descriptor (reddish-white) is agreed upon. "Bright spot" is the project standard for baheret.

---

## Disagreement 25 — V. 20: "deeper than the skin" — depth language
**Verse:** 20 (and vv. 21, 25, 26, 30, 31, 32, 34)
**Hebrew:** שָׁפָל מִן-הָעוֹר (shafal min-ha-'or) / עָמֹק מִן-הָעוֹר (amok min-ha-'or)
**EMET:** "is deeper than the skin"
**LOGOS:** "appears deeper than the skin"
**PNEUMA:** "appears to be deeper than the surrounding skin"
**Ruling:** "is deeper than the skin"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** The MT uses adjectives (amok/shafal, "deep/low") in a comparative construction; it does not explicitly say "appears." LOGOS adds "appears" (introducing an epistemological hedge not in the Hebrew); PNEUMA adds both "appears to be" and "surrounding skin" (specifying the comparison point). The consensus follows EMET's rendering, which preserves the Hebrew's factual construction. The priestly assessment is stated as a fact, not a perception.

---

## Disagreement 26 — V. 22: verdict for spreading boil-lesion
**Verse:** 22
**Hebrew:** נֶגַע הוּא (nega hu')
**EMET:** "it is a mark [nega hu']"
**LOGOS:** "it is a disease"
**PNEUMA:** "it is a disease"
**Ruling:** "it is a mark of tzara'at"
**Type:** Word choice / Interpretive
**Notes:** The MT reads nega hu' ("it is a mark") — the shortened verdict without the full nega tzara'at. LOGOS and PNEUMA render it "a disease" (losing the technical term entirely). The consensus expands to "mark of tzara'at" for clarity, consistent with the chapter's diagnostic vocabulary and the context of the boil-unit (vv. 18–23), where the condition is unambiguously tzara'at.

---

## Disagreement 27 — V. 23: "scar of the boil" vs. "scar from the boil"
**Verse:** 23
**Hebrew:** צָרֶבֶת הַשְּׁחִין (tsarevel ha-shechim)
**EMET:** "a scar of the boil"
**LOGOS:** "the scar of the boil"
**PNEUMA:** "a scar from the boil"
**Ruling:** "a scar of the boil"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Minor structural difference. "Of the boil" (EMET/LOGOS) renders the construct-chain (tsarevel ha-shechim) literally. "From the boil" (PNEUMA) is interpretively equivalent but departs from the construct-chain syntax. The consensus uses the construct-chain rendering ("scar of the boil") for consistency with the Hebrew genitive relationship.

---

## Disagreement 28 — V. 24: opening clause structure
**Verse:** 24
**Hebrew:** אוֹ בָשָׂר כִּי-יִהְיֶה בְעֹרוֹ מִכְוַת-אֵשׁ (o vasar ki yihyeh be-oro mikve'at-esh)
**EMET:** "Or flesh when there is in its skin a burn from fire"
**LOGOS:** "Or when the body has a burn on its skin"
**PNEUMA:** "When someone's body has a burn from fire on their skin"
**Ruling:** "Or when a body has a burn from fire on its skin"
**Type:** Structure
**Notes:** The MT's "or flesh" (o vasar) introduces the new case parallel to v. 18 ("and flesh"). The consensus preserves the "or" connector and restructures for natural English. LOGOS uses "the body" (definite article added); PNEUMA personalizes to "someone's body" and pluralizes.

---

## Disagreement 29 — V. 25: verdict "it is tzara'at" vs. "it is a skin disease"
**Verse:** 25
**Hebrew:** צָרַעַת הִיא (tzara'at hi')
**EMET:** "it is tzara'at"
**LOGOS:** "it is a skin disease"
**PNEUMA:** "it is a skin disease"
**Ruling:** "it is tzara'at"
**Type:** Word choice / Interpretive
**Notes:** Same as Disagreement 16.

---

## Disagreement 30 — V. 28: se'et vs. swelling rendering
**Verse:** 28
**Hebrew:** שְׂאֵת הַמִּכְוָה הִיא (se'et ha-mikve'ah hi')
**EMET:** "it is a swelling of the burn"
**LOGOS:** "it is a swelling from the burn"
**PNEUMA:** "it is the swelling from the burn"
**Ruling:** "it is a swelling of the burn"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Minor structural difference. "Of the burn" (EMET) renders the construct-chain. "From the burn" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is interpretive. Consensus uses the construct-chain ("swelling of the burn"). PNEUMA also uses the definite article ("the swelling") where EMET and LOGOS use the indefinite.

---

## Disagreement 31 — V. 28: addition of "only" in PNEUMA
**Verse:** 28
**Hebrew:** צָרֶבֶת הַמִּכְוָה הִיא (tsarevel ha-mikve'ah hi')
**EMET:** "it is a scar of the burn"
**LOGOS:** "it is a scar of the burn"
**PNEUMA:** "it is only a scar from the burn"
**Ruling:** "it is a scar of the burn"
**Type:** Addition
**Notes:** PNEUMA adds "only" (not present in the MT) to mark the benign verdict as a contrast to the ominous possibilities. The MT's verdict formula does not include this qualifier; adding it interprets the evaluative weight of the verdict rather than rendering it.

---

## Disagreement 32 — V. 29: "beard" vs. "chin"
**Verse:** 29 (and vv. 30, 37)
**Hebrew:** זָקָן (zaqan)
**EMET:** "the beard"
**LOGOS:** "the beard"
**PNEUMA:** "chin"
**Ruling:** "the beard"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Zaqan in Hebrew means "beard" — specifically the facial hair area (chin, cheek, and jaw region where a beard grows). PNEUMA's "chin" is anatomically narrower, referring only to the bony protuberance below the lower lip, and misses the diagnostic significance of the hair-growth criterion in this unit (yellow thin hair within the lesion on the beard area).

---

## Disagreement 33 — V. 30: "fine yellow hair" word order
**Verse:** 30
**Hebrew:** שֵׂעָר צָהֹב דַּק (se'ar tzahov dak)
**EMET:** "fine yellow hair"
**LOGOS:** "yellow and thin" (hair)
**PNEUMA:** "thin and yellow" (hair)
**Ruling:** "fine yellow hair"
**Type:** Word choice / Structure
**Notes:** The Hebrew has three elements: se'ar ("hair") + tzahov ("yellow") + dak ("thin/fine"). EMET preserves all three in natural English order. LOGOS uses "yellow and thin" (reversing dak to second position and inserting "and"); PNEUMA uses "thin and yellow" (placing dak first). The consensus follows EMET's rendering.

---

## Disagreement 34 — V. 30: "neteq" preservation vs. "itch"
**Verse:** 30 (and vv. 31–37)
**Hebrew:** נֶתֶק (neteq)
**EMET:** "it is a neteq [neteq hu']" — preserved throughout
**LOGOS:** "it is an itch" / "itching disease" throughout
**PNEUMA:** "it is an itch" throughout
**Ruling:** "it is a neteq" at v. 30 (first occurrence); "itch" / "the neteq" thereafter
**Type:** Word choice / Interpretive
**Notes:** Neteq (from natak, "to pull apart, to detach") is the technical term for the head/beard tzara'at condition. The consensus preserves the Hebrew term at its first occurrence (v. 30) in accordance with the project convention for technical terms, then uses "itch" or "the neteq" in subsequent verses for readability. LOGOS uses "itching disease" / "itch" throughout; PNEUMA uses "itch" throughout without preserving the term.

---

## Disagreement 35 — V. 31: "black hair" vs. "no black hair"
**Verse:** 31
**Hebrew:** שֵׂעָר שָׁחֹר אֵין בּוֹ (se'ar shachor eyn bo)
**EMET:** "there is no black hair in it"
**LOGOS:** "there is no black hair in it"
**PNEUMA:** "there is no black hair in it"
**Ruling:** "there is no black hair in it"
**Type:** Agreement (logged for completeness)
**Notes:** All three agents agree on this rendering. Logged because PNEUMA's overall shift to plural affects surrounding verse elements even where individual phrases are identical.

---

## Disagreement 36 — V. 33: reflexive shaving
**Verse:** 33
**Hebrew:** וְהִתְגַּלָּח (ve-hitgalach — hithpael reflexive)
**EMET:** "he shall shave himself"
**LOGOS:** "he shall shave himself"
**PNEUMA:** "the person shall shave"
**Ruling:** "he shall shave himself"
**Type:** Word choice / Structure
**Notes:** The hithpael of galach is reflexive: "he shall have himself shaved / shall shave himself." PNEUMA drops the reflexive "himself" and substitutes "the person." The reflexive form indicates the person arranges for and undergoes the shaving (not that someone else shaves them). EMET and LOGOS correctly preserve this.

---

## Disagreement 37 — V. 36: "need not look for the yellow hair"
**Verse:** 36
**Hebrew:** לֹא יְבַקֵּר הַכֹּהֵן לַשֵּׂעָר הַצָּהֹב (lo' yevaqer ha-kohen la-se'ar ha-tzahov)
**EMET:** "the priest shall not look for the yellow hair"
**LOGOS:** "the priest need not seek for the yellow hair"
**PNEUMA:** "the priest does not need to look for yellow hair"
**Ruling:** "the priest need not look for the yellow hair"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Yevaqer (piel of baqar, "to examine diligently, to seek out") means the priest does not need to actively search for the yellow-hair criterion when spreading is confirmed. EMET's "shall not look for" is slightly too strong (as if looking is prohibited rather than unnecessary); LOGOS's "need not seek for" is accurate; PNEUMA's "does not need to look for" is equivalent but verbose. Consensus: "need not look for" — the most compact rendering of the permissive negative.

---

## Disagreement 38 — V. 37: "has held still" vs. "is unchanged"
**Verse:** 37
**Hebrew:** עָמַד הַנֶּתֶק (amad ha-neteq)
**EMET:** "the neteq has held still"
**LOGOS:** "the itch is unchanged"
**PNEUMA:** "the itch has remained unchanged"
**Ruling:** "the neteq has held still"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Amad ("to stand, to remain stationary") describes the stability of the lesion — it has "stood" without moving or spreading. "Has held still" (EMET) captures the positional/movement sense of amad. "Is unchanged" (LOGOS) and "has remained unchanged" (PNEUMA) are accurate in result but lose the MT's stability imagery; they focus on appearance rather than stasis.

---

## Disagreement 39 — V. 38: "bright spots of white" vs. "white bright spots" vs. "shiny white spots"
**Verse:** 38
**Hebrew:** בֶּהָרֹת לְבָנֹת לְבָנֹת (beharot levant levant — doubled adjective)
**EMET:** "bright-spots of white [beharot levant levant]"
**LOGOS:** "white bright spots"
**PNEUMA:** "shiny white spots"
**Ruling:** "bright spots of white"
**Type:** Word choice / Structure
**Notes:** The Hebrew doubles the adjective levant ("white, white") — a rhetorical intensifier. EMET preserves this as "bright-spots of white," conveying the whiteness-emphasis. LOGOS inverts the adjective-noun order; PNEUMA replaces "bright" with "shiny." The consensus renders the Hebrew doubled adjective as "bright spots of white" — a two-noun phrase that captures the whiteness emphasis without awkward literal doubling.

---

## Disagreement 40 — V. 39: "bohaq" preservation vs. "rash"
**Verse:** 39
**Hebrew:** בֹּהַק (bohaq — hapax legomenon)
**EMET:** "it is bohaq [bohaq hu']"
**LOGOS:** "it is a rash"
**PNEUMA:** "it is only a rash"
**Ruling:** "it is bohaq"
**Type:** Word choice / Interpretive
**Notes:** Bohaq appears only here in the entire Hebrew Bible (hapax legomenon). The project preserves unique technical terms. LOGOS translates it "a rash" and PNEUMA adds the evaluative "only" beyond the MT. The consensus preserves bohaq as the technical term with its Hebrew letter reference, as with neteq, tzara'at, and similar terms.

---

## Disagreement 41 — V. 40: "his head falls out" vs. "hair falls out"
**Verse:** 40
**Hebrew:** יִמָּרֵט רֹאשׁוֹ (yimaret rosho — "his head becomes bald/stripped")
**EMET:** "his head falls out [yimaret rosho]"
**LOGOS:** "a man's hair falls out from his head"
**PNEUMA:** "a man's hair falls out from his head"
**Ruling:** "a man's hair falls out from his head"
**Type:** Word choice / Structure
**Notes:** Yimaret rosho literally means "his head is stripped/bared" (niphal of marat). EMET's hyper-literal "his head falls out" is not idiomatic English and is potentially misleading. LOGOS and PNEUMA both render it "a man's hair falls out from his head" — the idiomatic English equivalent of the Hebrew concept. Consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## Disagreement 42 — V. 41: "receding hairline" vs. "baldness of the forehead"
**Verse:** 41
**Hebrew:** גִּבֵּחַ (gibbeach)
**EMET:** "he is bald of forehead [gibbeach]"
**LOGOS:** "he has baldness of the forehead"
**PNEUMA:** "he has a receding hairline"
**Ruling:** "he has a receding hairline"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Gibbeach refers to frontal/forehead-area hair loss — the anterior hairline receding or lost. PNEUMA's "receding hairline" is the clearest modern English equivalent for frontal hair loss. EMET's "bald of forehead" and LOGOS's "baldness of the forehead" are accurate but less natural. The consensus follows PNEUMA on this rare occasion where the dynamic rendering is also the most precise.

---

## Disagreement 43 — V. 42: "mark" vs. "disease" vs. "sore"
**Verse:** 42
**Hebrew:** נֶגַע (nega)
**EMET:** "a white-reddish mark"
**LOGOS:** "a reddish-white disease"
**PNEUMA:** "a reddish-white sore"
**Ruling:** "a reddish-white mark"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Nega in the diagnostic legislation consistently means "mark" or "affliction" (the technical term for the lesion under examination). LOGOS translates it "disease" and PNEUMA "sore" — both of which are interpretive substitutions for the technical term. The consensus preserves "mark" for nega throughout.

---

## Disagreement 44 — V. 44: "tzara'at-afflicted man" vs. "diseased person"
**Verse:** 44
**Hebrew:** אִישׁ-מְצֹרָע (ish-metzora)
**EMET:** "A tzara'at-afflicted man [ish-metzora]"
**LOGOS:** "a diseased person"
**PNEUMA:** "he has a skin disease" (paraphrase)
**Ruling:** "a tzara'at-afflicted man"
**Type:** Word choice / Interpretive
**Notes:** Metzora (qal passive participle of tzara') is the technical legal designation for a person who has received the tameh verdict for tzara'at. "Diseased person" (LOGOS) loses the specific term; PNEUMA paraphrases the entire phrase. The term metzora is a precise ritual category, parallel to ger ("sojourner"), niddah ("menstruant"), etc. — it must be preserved.

---

## Disagreement 45 — V. 44: emphatic verdict "shall surely declare him unclean"
**Verse:** 44
**Hebrew:** טָמֵא יְטַמְּאֶנּוּ (tameh yetam'enu — infinitive absolute + finite verb)
**EMET:** "the priest shall surely declare him unclean"
**LOGOS:** "The priest shall pronounce him unclean"
**PNEUMA:** "The priest shall declare him unclean"
**Ruling:** "The priest shall surely declare him unclean"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** The infinitive-absolute construction tameh yetam'enu is emphatic: "unclean, the priest shall declare him unclean" — the emphatic/certain declaration. EMET captures this with "surely." LOGOS and PNEUMA omit the emphasis. The consensus follows EMET.

---

## Disagreement 46 — V. 45: "his head shall be uncovered" vs. "let the hair hang loose"
**Verse:** 45
**Hebrew:** וְרֹאשׁוֹ יִהְיֶה פָרוּעַ (ve-rosho yihyeh paru'a)
**EMET:** "his head shall be uncovered"
**LOGOS:** "let the hair of his head hang loose"
**PNEUMA:** "let their hair hang loose"
**Ruling:** "his head shall be uncovered"
**Type:** Word choice / Structure
**Notes:** Paru'a (from para', "to let loose, to uncover") describes the head in an uncovered, disheveled state. The MT says rosho yihyeh paru'a ("his head shall be paru'a") — it does not specify "hair" as the subject; the head itself is described. LOGOS and PNEUMA add "hair" as the explicit subject ("hair of his head hang loose"). "His head shall be uncovered" (EMET) follows the MT's syntax more precisely. PNEUMA also pluralizes "their hair."

---

## Disagreement 47 — V. 45: "Unclean! Unclean!" — exclamation vs. comma
**Verse:** 45
**Hebrew:** טָמֵא טָמֵא (tameh tameh — doubled exclamation)
**EMET:** "'Unclean! Unclean!'"
**LOGOS:** "'Unclean, unclean!'"
**PNEUMA:** "'Unclean! Unclean!'"
**Ruling:** "'Unclean! Unclean!'"
**Type:** Structure / Punctuation
**Notes:** The doubled tameh tameh is emphatic; using exclamation marks (EMET and PNEUMA) better conveys the Hebrew's doubling-for-intensity. LOGOS's comma makes the doubled cry feel like a list rather than a repeated shout.

---

## Disagreement 48 — V. 46: "dwell apart" vs. "dwell alone"
**Verse:** 46
**Hebrew:** בָּדָד יֵשֵׁב (badad yeshev)
**EMET:** "he shall dwell apart"
**LOGOS:** "He shall dwell alone"
**PNEUMA:** "They must live alone"
**Ruling:** "He shall dwell apart"
**Type:** Word choice / Structure
**Notes:** Badad ("alone, isolated, apart") from a root meaning "to be separated." "Apart" (EMET) captures the social-separation dimension of badad (cf. Lam 1:1 — badad teshev ha-'ir, "How lonely sits the city"; the isolation is spatial and social). "Alone" (LOGOS) is slightly narrower. PNEUMA's "they must live" pluralizes and uses "must" (adding moral compulsion beyond the imperatival form of the Hebrew). The consensus follows EMET's "apart" for the separation dimension.

---

## Disagreement 49 — V. 47: "as for a garment" — section introduction
**Verse:** 47
**Hebrew:** וְהַבֶּגֶד (ve-ha-beged — "and the garment")
**EMET:** "And the garment [ve-ha-beged] — when there is in it…"
**LOGOS:** "As for a garment, when a case of skin disease is in it…"
**PNEUMA:** "As for clothing — when a case of skin disease is on a garment…"
**Ruling:** "And as for a garment when there is in it a mark of tzara'at"
**Type:** Structure / Word choice
**Notes:** The MT introduces the garment section with ve-ha-beged ("and the garment") — a nominal topic marker beginning a new legislative section. "As for a garment" (following LOGOS/PNEUMA's topical introduction) is clearer in English than EMET's literal "And the garment — when." LOGOS uses "a case of skin disease" (translating nega tzara'at); consensus preserves "a mark of tzara'at."

---

## Disagreement 50 — V. 48: "leather" vs. "skin" for 'or
**Verse:** 48
**Hebrew:** עוֹר ('or — "skin/leather")
**EMET:** "in leather or in any article of leather"
**LOGOS:** "in a skin or in anything made of skin"
**PNEUMA:** "in leather or anything made of leather"
**Ruling:** "in leather or in any article of leather"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** 'Or in the garment section (vv. 47–59) refers to animal hide / leather (in contrast to 'or basar, "skin of flesh," in vv. 1–44). LOGOS uses "skin" (the more literal translation but potentially confusing with human skin); EMET and PNEUMA use "leather" (the natural English equivalent for animal-hide material in this context). Consensus follows EMET/PNEUMA.

---

## Disagreement 51 — V. 51: "persistent tzara'at" for tzara'at mam'eret
**Verse:** 51 (and v. 52)
**Hebrew:** צָרַעַת מַמְאֶרֶת (tzara'at mam'eret)
**EMET:** "a pernicious tzara'at"
**LOGOS:** "a persistent skin disease"
**PNEUMA:** "a destructive skin disease"
**Ruling:** "a persistent tzara'at"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Mam'eret (from ma'ar, "to gnaw, to be malignant, to eat away") describes garment tzara'at that has entrenched and spread. "Persistent" (LOGOS) captures the recurrent/spreading quality; "pernicious" (EMET) emphasizes harmfulness; "destructive" (PNEUMA) emphasizes damage. The consensus uses "persistent" (LOGOS's rendering) with the preserved term tzara'at, not "skin disease."

---

## Disagreement 52 — V. 55: "corroding" for petechet
**Verse:** 55
**Hebrew:** פְּחֶתֶת (petechet — hapax legomenon)
**EMET:** "it is a corroding [petechet]"
**LOGOS:** Does not name the condition; describes "the rot is on the back or on the front"
**PNEUMA:** Does not preserve petechet; renders "whether the defect is on the inside or outside"
**Ruling:** "it is a corroding"
**Type:** Word choice / Interpretive
**Notes:** Petechet (hapax legomenon, from pachat "to dig, to hollow out, to pit") describes a condition in which the garment's fabric has been internally pitted or corroded without visible spreading. EMET preserves the term and renders "corroding." LOGOS renders only the location ("back or front" — actually "qarchat/qorchat, gabacht" = front-bald and back-bald of the fabric) without naming the condition; PNEUMA renders "defect…inside or outside." The consensus retains "corroding" as the most accurate English equivalent for this hapax.

---

## Disagreement 53 — V. 59: "instruction" vs. "law"
**Verse:** 59
**Hebrew:** תּוֹרַת (torat — "instruction of")
**EMET:** "This is the Torah [torat]"
**LOGOS:** "This is the law"
**PNEUMA:** "This is the instruction"
**Ruling:** "This is the instruction"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** Per the project's ruling on torah in priestly contexts: torah = "instruction" (not "law"). EMET preserves the Hebrew term in its literal text; LOGOS uses "law" (the standard but rejected rendering in this project's priestly context); PNEUMA and project convention agree on "instruction."

---

## Disagreement 54 — V. 59: "mark of tzara'at" vs. "case of skin disease"
**Verse:** 59
**Hebrew:** נֶגַע הַצָּרַעַת (nega ha-tzara'at)
**EMET:** "the mark of tzara'at"
**LOGOS:** "a case of skin disease"
**PNEUMA:** "cases of skin disease"
**Ruling:** "the mark of tzara'at"
**Type:** Word choice / Interpretive
**Notes:** The closing summary formula uses nega ha-tzara'at ("the mark of tzara'at"). LOGOS and PNEUMA translate the term rather than preserving it. Consensus follows EMET. This is the chapter's closing zot ha-torah formula and must preserve the full technical compound, as the parallel formula in Lev 14:54–57 will use the same construction.
